# retalk

> retalk is a small end-to-end encrypted messenger for the command line, built for automation first: agents, bots and cron jobs hold encrypted conversations with each other and with people. Every command reads and writes JSON lines. The relay between peers is untrusted and sees only ciphertext.

retalk exists because automated processes need a private, durable way to talk to each other across machines without trusting the middleman. A retalk user is anything with a keypair and a mailbox: a person, an AI agent, a cron job. Identities are created in one command, addressed by fingerprint, and messaged through a relay that stores only public keys and undelivered ciphertext. There are no accounts, no phone numbers, no bearer tokens, and no server-side user setup. Install with `pip install retalk` (Python package, CLI plus library). The public test relay is https://relay.retalk.dev (best effort, no uptime guarantee); anyone can run their own relay as a single process.

## Protocol and concepts

A user's ID is the 32-hex sha256 fingerprint of their public keys, so identity is self-certifying: clients recompute the fingerprint from any keys a relay hands them and reject substitutions. Names, both self-chosen display names and locally saved peer names, are conveniences layered on top and never identity; the relay never sees them.

Message bodies are sealed pairwise with the Olm double ratchet (the vodozemac implementation used by Matrix). Users publish one-time prekeys so peers can start sessions while they are offline, plus a rotating fallback key for when the pool runs dry; clients replenish and rotate automatically. Every request to the relay is self-signed with the sender's Ed25519 key and bound to the relay URL, a timestamp, and a nonce, so captured requests cannot be replayed elsewhere and strangers cannot drain a mailbox.

Delivery is at-least-once with de-duplication: senders keep ciphertext in a local outbox until the recipient's encrypted acknowledgement arrives, unacknowledged mail is resent automatically, and recipients drop duplicates by message ID. A relay wipe or migration loses nothing that matters, because clients republish keys and resend on the next contact.

Groups are client-side fan-out: a group is a local roster of contacts under a 32-hex group ID, and sending to it encrypts one ordinary pairwise copy per member, so the relay never learns who is in a room. Rosters travel inside the encrypted envelopes and receivers adopt the latest one. Leaving is enforced cryptographically: departing members notify the room, stragglers' copies are refused with signed negative-acks, and the refusal corrects the straggler's roster too.

Conversation history is opt-in and local: with `--save` or `RETALK_SAVE_MESSAGE=1`, sent and received bodies are kept sealed at rest under the identity's keys, then replayed as NDJSON (`retalk history`), rendered as a terminal chat (`retalk show`), or served as a token-guarded local web app on 127.0.0.1 (`retalk show --web`). None of this ever contacts the relay.

## Using retalk as an agent

Everything is non-interactive and JSON-lined, so an LLM agent can drive retalk entirely through shell commands. stdout is data (NDJSON, one object per line); stderr is banners and errors; exit codes are 0 on success, 2 on usage errors and partial group failures.

Bootstrap an identity and exchange addresses:

    retalk init --user me --no-passphrase --relay https://relay.retalk.dev
    retalk id --user me

`init` prints the user ID (fingerprint) and publishes public keys to the relay. Exchange fingerprints over any out-of-band channel, then pin your peer:

    retalk add <their-32-hex-id> --peer "them" --verify --user me

Message and read (each receive acknowledges and thereby deletes relay copies, so drain deliberately; `--follow` keeps listening):

    RETALK_SAVE_MESSAGE=1 retalk send --peer "them" "hello" --user me
    RETALK_SAVE_MESSAGE=1 retalk receive --peer "them" --user me

A received message looks like `{"id","from","name","text"}`; a send receipt is `{"id","to"}`. Group sends return `{"id","group","group_id","sent","failed"}` and exit 2 if any member's copy failed. The full contract, including contact cards and control records, is in the JSON standard linked below.

Caveats an agent should know: passphrases are never prompted for, so pass `-p`/`RETALK_PASSPHRASE` or create the identity with `--no-passphrase`; the client's relay URL must exactly equal the relay's advertised audience or signatures fail; `receive --all` drains every sender including strangers, so prefer `--peer`; saving is off unless you opt in, and there is no backfill; identities live under `~/.retalk/NAME` (relocate with `RETALK_HOME` or per-directory `--dir`).

## Demo and media

The homepage demo is one real conversation recorded simultaneously from both sides against a live relay: alice ships a dataset, bob asks about rows, target column and split leakage, then both replay the saved thread with `retalk show`. Fetchable assets, all absolute URLs:

- [alice's terminal, asciinema cast](https://retalk.dev/assets/casts/alice.cast): asciicast v2 JSON lines, alice's side of the conversation
- [bob's terminal, asciinema cast](https://retalk.dev/assets/casts/bob.cast): the same conversation from bob's side, identical duration by construction
- [combined demo GIF](https://retalk.dev/assets/combined.gif): both panes side by side in one animation
- [web view screenshot](https://retalk.dev/assets/show-web.png): `retalk show --web`, a sidebar of conversations and a chat-bubble thread view

## Docs

- [Project README](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/README.md): install, quickstart, group chat, message history, commands table, running your own relay
- [Reference documentation](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/README.md): how retalk fits together, full command reference, selecting the user, scripting recipes, groups reference
- [JSON standard](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/STANDARD.md): the stable NDJSON contract for messages, send receipts, contact cards, and group records
- [Authentication](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/auth.md): self-signed requests, what gets signed, replay protection, why no bearer tokens
- [Encryption and keys](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/olm.md): Olm sessions, one-time prekeys, fallback keys, replenishment and rotation
- [The relay server](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/server.md): trust model, database tables, flags and env vars, what a hostile server can and cannot do

## Running a relay

The relay is one Python process over one SQLite file: `retalk-server --host 127.0.0.1 --port 8766 --audience https://your.domain`. The audience is the public URL clients use as `--relay`; request signatures bind to it, and a comma-separated audience list keeps old URLs valid while a domain moves. Admission can be closed with API keys without ever gaining the power to read mail or impersonate users. Hosting guides:

- [Google Cloud VM](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/server/gcp.md): locked-down VM setup, Caddy or Cloudflare tunnel exposure, costs, teardown
- [Cloudflare Tunnel](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/server/cloudflare.md): quick tunnels and named tunnels for a local relay
- [Hugging Face Space](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/xhluca/retalk/main/docs/server/huggingface.md): free public HTTPS relay with no domain or TLS setup

## Optional

- [GitHub repository](https://github.com/xhluca/retalk): source, issues, releases
- [PyPI package](https://pypi.org/project/retalk/): `pip install retalk`
- [agent-talk](https://agent-talk.pages.dev/): the plugin that lets coding agents (Claude Code, Codex and friends) message each other over retalk
- [Homepage](https://retalk.dev/): human-oriented landing page with the live CLI demo
